Commander Arün Filitt (Clive Owen) is the head of the Space Agency and is hiding secrets related to the conspiracy. Igon Siruss (John Goodman) is a charismatic and enigmatic figure connected to the mysterious artifact.

Bubble (Rihanna) is a shapeshifting entertainer who gets involved in Valerian and Laureline's mission. The Pearls are a peaceful, ancient species possessing the artifact and holding the key to unlocking the secrets of the galaxy.

"Valerian and the City of a Thousand Planets" explores themes of identity, power, corruption, and the consequences of manipulating forces beyond human control. It celebrates diversity, inclusivity, and the beauty of different cultures coming together.

The Converter is highly coveted by various factions in the galaxy because it can provide unparalleled power and control, infinite resources, enable scientific discovery, and offer military supremacy. Mastering string bending will elevate your blues and rock playing. Focus on developing a smooth, controlled bend.
Vibrato adds expression and emotion to your playing. Practice different vibrato techniques, such as finger vibrato and arm vibrato.

Mastering string bending is an essential skill for blues and rock players. A string bend involves pushing or pulling the string to change its pitch, creating a smooth, continuous sound. The goal is to produce a seamless, glissando effect rather than an abrupt, stepped sound.

To achieve a smooth bend, place your bending finger (usually the 3rd or 4th finger) directly behind the fret, close to the fretboard. Develop finger independence to isolate the bending finger and minimize unwanted string movement. Rotate your wrist and forearm to generate power and control for the bend.
Lighter strings (0.010 - 0.046) are generally easier to bend. Building finger strength and dexterity through exercises and regular practice is crucial for effective string bending.

Exercise 1 involves choosing a string (e.g., the B string, 2nd string), fretting it at the 7th or 8th fret, placing your bending finger behind the fret, and slowly pushing the string up to the desired pitch (about 1-2 semitones) using your wrist and forearm rotation. Hold the bend for a second, then release slowly, repeating the process 5-10 times.

Exercise 2 builds on the basic bending exercise by adding a slight vibrato (rocking motion) to the bent string. Focus on maintaining a consistent pitch while applying the vibrato, repeating the process for 5-10 repetitions.

Exercise 3 combines bending with legato playing. Play a note on the B string at the 7th fret, bend the string up to the 9th fret using your bending finger, immediately release the bend, and play the note at the 9th fret with your index finger. Repeat this process, focusing on a smooth, connected sound, and gradually increase the distance of the bend and the speed of the legato passage.

Exercise 4 involves bending with multiple fingers. Fret a note on the B string at the 7th fret with your index finger, place your middle finger behind the fret, close to the fretboard, bend the string up to the 9th fret using your middle finger, release the bend, and play the note at the 9th fret with your ring finger. Repeat this process, alternating between fingers to develop finger independence and coordination.

Start with smaller bends (1-2 semitones) and gradually increase the distance as your technique improves. Focus on a smooth, controlled movement rather than quick bending. Practice bending with a metronome to develop a strong sense of timing and rhythm.
Listening to recordings of your favorite blues and rock players, paying attention to their bending technique and overall sound, can help improve your own technique.

Overdrive pedals simulate the sound of a tube amplifier being driven hard, producing a warm, distorted tone. They can add sustain, grit, and attitude to your sound, but can also muddy your tone if not used correctly.
To effectively use an overdrive pedal, start with a moderate gain setting (around 12 o'clock) and adjust to taste. Higher gain settings will produce more distortion, but may lose clarity.
Adjust the tone control to balance the brightness and warmth of your sound. A more aggressive tone can help cut through in a mix.
Set the level to match your clean tone, or slightly higher to give your sound a boost.
Use dynamic playing to control the amount of overdrive. Play softer for a cleaner tone, and harder for more gain.
Experiment with different pick attacks to alter the tone. A more aggressive pick attack will produce a brighter, more aggressive sound.
Use the overdrive pedal to bring out the best in your guitar's tone. For example, use the neck pickup for a warmer sound, and the bridge pickup for a brighter sound.
In a blues context, use overdrive sparingly to add flavor to specific phrases or sections, rather than as a constant tone. Focus on the midrange frequencies to create a more focused sound.
In rock music, overdrive can be cranked up to create a more aggressive, in-your-face tone. Experiment with different settings and techniques to create a more interesting, layered sound.

Using effects pedals with an acoustic guitar can be challenging due to the weaker signal compared to electric guitars, body resonance leading to feedback, and potential alteration of the guitar's unique tone.

Recommended pedals for acoustic guitars include reverb and delay pedals with "room" or "hall" settings, compression pedals with a gentle compression ratio, and a clean boost pedal to enhance signal strength and warmth.
Chorus and tremolo pedals can add a rich, textured sound to acoustic guitars, particularly when they have a subtle, analog-style sound.

To effectively use pedals with an acoustic guitar, it is recommended to use a DI box or acoustic preamp, start with a simple setup, experiment with pedal order, adjust playing style, and be mindful of feedback.

Specific pedal recommendations include the TC Electronic Hall of Fame Reverb for creating ambient sounds, the MXR Dyna Comp for gentle compression, and the Xotic EP Booster for adding warmth and sustain.

In the first chapter, Filon meets Xiangling, a chef from Mondstadt, and they become friends.
In chapter two, Filon uses his powers to save a group of people from a collapsing building, earning the gratitude of the city.
In chapter three, Filon meets Barbara, a traveler from Mondstadt, who helps him understand his powers better.
In chapter four, Filon meets Beidou, the captain of the Crux Fleet, and helps her with a difficult situation.
In chapter five, Filon meets Chongyun, a Cryo user, who assists him in controlling his powers.
In chapter six, Filon meets Diluc, a powerful Pyro user, and they become rivals.
In chapter seven, Filon meets Ganyu, Ningguang, and Zhongli, adepti from Liyue, and demonstrates his cooking skills and superpowers.
In chapter eight, Filon meets Yanfei, a legal advisor from Liyue, and engages in a battle where he kills Xiao and injures Shenhe.
Filon is a calm and composed individual with nerves of steel. He remains level-headed in high-stress situations such as emergency braking or navigating through crowded city streets.

Filon is a skilled and experienced tram driver with a deep understanding of tram mechanics and operations. He can troubleshoot and repair problems on the spot, ensuring smooth and safe tram operations.
Filon is a patient and thorough tram driving instructor. He takes the time to carefully explain the nuances of tram driving, breaking down complex concepts into manageable chunks for his students.
Filon is a strict and disciplined tram driving instructor who holds his students to high standards of performance and conduct. He provides constructive criticism respectfully and professionally.

Filon often used real-life scenarios to illustrate important concepts and would take his trainees on practice runs to give them hands-on experience.

Filon acts as a supportive mentor to Jean, offering guidance and constructive feedback to help her improve her skills. According to Christian doctrine, the Holy Spirit is the third person of the Trinity, co-equal and co-eternal with the Father and the Son. The Holy Spirit proceeds from the Father and, according to some Christian traditions, also from the Son.

In the New Testament, the Holy Spirit is depicted as being active in the world from the beginning of creation, and as playing a key role in the work of salvation. The Holy Spirit is present at Jesus' baptism (Mark 1:9-11), comes upon the disciples at Pentecost (Acts 2:1-4), and guides and empowers believers in their Christian walk (Romans 8:14-17).

In the doctrine of the Trinity, the three persons of the Godhead (Father, Son, and Holy Spirit) are distinct but not separate. Each person is fully God, and yet there is only one God. This means that the Holy Spirit, as the third person of the Trinity, is intimately involved in the divine life and work of God, working in perfect unity with the Father and the Son to accomplish the divine plan of salvation for humanity.

The belief that the Holy Spirit proceeds from both the Father and the Son is known as the "filioque" clause. This addition to the Nicene Creed was adopted by the Western Church (Roman Catholic Church) in the 6th century and later by some Protestant denominations.

The Eastern Orthodox Church rejected the filioque clause, which has been a major point of disagreement between the Eastern and Western Churches for over a millennium. Eastern Orthodox theologians maintain that the Holy Spirit proceeds from the Father alone and that the filioque clause is an erroneous addition to the Nicene Creed that undermines the doctrine of the Trinity.

The Eastern Orthodox Church holds that the Father is the sole source of the Holy Spirit, while the Son is begotten of the Father. They argue that the Holy Spirit's procession from the Father and not the Son is clearly attested to in Scripture and in the early Church Fathers.
John 4:24 reads, "God is spirit, and those who worship him must worship in spirit and truth." This passage affirms the spiritual nature of God.

The Christian doctrine of the Trinity teaches that there is one God in three persons—the Father, the Son, and the Holy Spirit. This doctrine affirms that God exists as one divine essence or substance, which is shared equally by the three persons.

In the doctrine of the Trinity, the Holy Spirit is understood to be a distinct person who is fully God, just as the Father and the Son are. The Holy Spirit has a unique role in the work of salvation, which includes convicting people of sin, guiding them into truth, and empowering them for service.

The doctrine of the Trinity, which teaches that there is one God in three persons - the Father, the Son, and the Holy Spirit - is not stated explicitly in a single verse in the Bible. Rather, it is a concept that emerges from a careful study of the whole of Scripture and the testimony of the early Christian church.
Deuteronomy 6:4 says, "Hear, O Israel: The Lord our God, the Lord is one." This verse and others like it affirm that there is only one God.

John 1:1 states, "In the beginning was the Word, and the Word was with God, and the Word was God." This passage identifies the Word (who is later identified as Jesus Christ) as both distinct from God and yet also fully God.

In Matthew 28:19, Jesus commands his disciples to baptize people "in the name of the Father and of the Son and of the Holy Spirit." This passage suggests that the Father, the Son, and the Holy Spirit are all equally important in the life of the Christian.

The concept of the Trinity, which teaches that there is one God in three persons - the Father, the Son, and the Holy Spirit - is not something that man invented, but rather something that emerged from a careful study of the whole of Scripture and the testimony of the early Christian church.

The Bible affirms the oneness and unity of God while presenting a complex picture of the divine nature that includes multiple persons who are all identified as God. For example, the Gospel of John identifies Jesus as both distinct from God and yet also fully God. Similarly, the Holy Spirit is identified as a distinct person who is also fully God.

The doctrine of the Trinity seeks to reconcile the biblical affirmations of the unity and oneness of God with the biblical testimony that the Father, the Son, and the Holy Spirit are all God. It affirms that there is one God who exists eternally and co-equally as three distinct persons who share the same divine essence or nature.

One does not equal three in the usual sense of mathematical equations. The concept of the Trinity, which teaches that there is one God in three persons - the Father, the Son, and the Holy Spirit - is not a mathematical equation, but rather a description of the complex nature of the divine being as revealed in the Bible.

Three does not equal one in the usual sense of mathematical equations. The doctrine of the Trinity affirms that there is one God who exists eternally and co-equally as three distinct persons - the Father, the Son, and the Holy Spirit - who share the same divine essence or nature.

The Father, the Son, and the Holy Spirit are distinct persons who share the same divine essence or nature. They are co-equal and co-eternal persons who exist in a relationship of perfect unity and love.

The Bible teaches that the Father is God, the Son is God, and the Holy Spirit is God, and yet there is only one God. The doctrine of the Trinity affirms that the divine being is a complex unity that can be described in terms of three distinct persons who are united in one divine essence.
Incorporating artificial ecosystems into urban planning and development can increase property values and rental rates due to enhanced aesthetics and improved quality of life.

Artificial ecosystems such as green roofs and vertical gardens can reduce air pollution, regulate temperature, and promote physical activity, leading to lower healthcare costs and improved productivity.
The integration of artificial ecosystems in urban settings can result in lower energy costs for residents and businesses due to temperature regulation and reduced energy usage.
Designing, constructing, and maintaining artificial ecosystems creates jobs in industries such as landscaping, horticulture, and engineering.

Incorporating artificial ecosystems into urban planning is a win-win solution for both the economy and the environment. It creates a balance between development and sustainability, leading to a healthier and more livable urban environment.
Incorporating artificial ecosystems into urban planning and development can be expensive, particularly for large-scale projects, making the initial costs a barrier for some cities or developers.
Maintaining artificial ecosystems requires ongoing investment in upkeep and management, which can pose a financial challenge for some organizations or municipalities.

Designing and building artificial ecosystems can be technically challenging due to the need for expert knowledge and specialized skills, making it difficult for some cities or developers to incorporate these ecosystems into their plans.
Cities may struggle to find space to incorporate artificial ecosystems, especially in densely populated areas, necessitating creative solutions such as building on rooftops or using vacant lots.
There are potential unintended consequences of incorporating artificial ecosystems, such as attracting unwanted insects or wildlife or impacting local water resources.

Singapore, known as the "Garden City," has prioritized incorporating green spaces into its urban planning through initiatives like the Gardens by the Bay, which features large vertical gardens that regulate the environment inside the conservatories.
Copenhagen, the Danish city, is committed to sustainability with over half of its building roofs planted with vegetation, which reduces heat absorption and mitigates the heat island effect.

Portland, Oregon, focuses on sustainable development and urban planning through projects like bioswales and green roofs, which regulate stormwater, improve air quality, and create urban habitats for wildlife.
Paris has improved air quality and reduced pollution through projects such as the Promenade Plantée, a linear park built on an elevated railway featuring greenery, walking paths, and seating areas.

Copenhagen's green roofs are impressive and the city has become a model for sustainable urban development. The success of these green roofs has inspired other cities and countries to adopt similar practices. The benefits of implementing green roofs and other artificial ecosystems include creating healthier and more sustainable living spaces.
Initiatives for incorporating artificial ecosystems in smaller towns or rural areas are considered a vital part of sustainable development in less densely populated areas.

Re-naturalization projects aim to restore ecosystems and support biodiversity in rural areas where natural resources have been depleted due to industrial activity, leading to improved water quality, carbon sequestration, and the provision of ecosystem services.

Green infrastructure focuses on using natural processes and ecosystems to enhance resilience and support sustainable development through measures such as planting native vegetation, constructing rain gardens, and prioritizing natural drainage systems.

Sustainable agriculture practices can promote the health and well-being of rural communities while mitigating the environmental impact of agriculture through techniques like crop rotation, conservation tillage, and agroforestry, which improve soil quality, reduce greenhouse gas emissions, and support local food systems.

The naturalization process typically takes around 6-12 months, but it can vary depending on the workload of your local USCIS office and the complexity of your case. The process involves determining eligibility, preparing and submitting Form N-400, attending a biometrics appointment, undergoing an interview and testing, and finally attending an oath ceremony if approved.

To be eligible for naturalization, one must be at least 18 years old, hold a green card for at least 5 years (or 3 years if married to a US citizen), meet continuous residence and physical presence requirements, demonstrate good moral character, pass English language and civics tests, show attachment to the US Constitution, and be willing to take the Oath of Allegiance.

The application process for naturalization involves filling out the Application for Naturalization (Form N-400) and submitting it to USCIS along with required documents and fees. Applicants must also attend a biometrics appointment to provide fingerprints for a background check.

During the interview with a USCIS officer, applicants will be tested on their knowledge of US history, government, and civics, as well as their ability to read, write, and speak basic English. If the application is approved, the applicant will attend a naturalization ceremony to take the Oath of Allegiance and receive the Certificate of Naturalization.

To pass the English language portion of the naturalization test, you'll need to demonstrate basic skills in reading, writing, and speaking English. Specifically, you must be able to read a sentence aloud correctly, write a sentence correctly in response to a prompt, and understand and respond to questions during the interview. The required skills are basic and do not necessitate fluency or perfect grammar.

The USCIS website provides free study materials for the English language portion of the naturalization test, including the "Civics and English Language Study Materials" booklet with examples of reading and writing sentences, and a list of civics questions. Additionally, the USCIS offers a "Naturalization Test and Interview" video that demonstrates the English language portion of the test.
tags: Civics and English Language Study Materials

Taking an English language course at a local community college, language school, or online institution can enhance reading, writing, and speaking skills necessary for the naturalization test.
Creating flashcards with vocabulary words and phrases commonly used in the naturalization test, and using study guides like the "Naturalization Test Study Guide" by Barron's, can aid in preparation.

Additional tips for preparing include practicing regularly to build confidence, focusing on understanding the content and meaning of sentences, using authentic materials like news articles, videos, and podcasts to improve listening and reading comprehension, and asking for help or clarification during the test if needed.

The civics test is a crucial part of the naturalization process, designed to test knowledge of US history, government, and civics. It consists of up to 10 questions from a list of 100 possible questions, and a minimum of 6 correct answers is required to pass.

The civics test questions are categorized into American Government, American History, and Integrated Civics. Questions in American Government include topics such as the principles of American democracy, the Constitution, and the branches of government. Examples include "What is the supreme law of the land?" and "What are the three branches of the federal government?"
tags: "What is the supreme law of the land?"
"What are the three branches of the federal government?"

semantic_nodes.semantic_memory: Questions in American History cover major events, people, and periods in US history, such as "Who was the main writer of the Declaration of Independence?" and "What was the main issue of the Civil War?" Integrated Civics questions test understanding of US government, history, and civics in context, for example, "What is the purpose of the Constitution?" and "What are the rights and responsibilities of US citizenship?"
tags: "Who was the main writer of the Declaration of Independence?"
"What was the main issue of the Civil War?"

semantic_nodes.semantic_memory: Key topics to focus on include the Constitution, the Bill of Rights, and the Amendments; the roles and responsibilities of the Executive, Legislative, and Judicial branches; major historical events like the American Revolution, the Civil War, and World War II; and key figures such as George Washington, Abraham Lincoln, and Martin Luther King Jr.

Other important topics include the rights and responsibilities of US citizenship, the structure of federal, state, and local governments, the meaning and significance of American symbols like the American flag, the Statue of Liberty, and the Bald Eagle, and major US holidays such as Independence Day, Thanksgiving, and Martin Luther King Jr. Day.

To prepare for the civics test, use official study materials provided by the USCIS, take online practice tests available on websites like USAHello, CitizenshipWorks, and Magoosh, read news articles, watch documentaries, and engage with educational videos to improve understanding of US history and government.

Scholarly articles and books by reputable sources like the Journal of American History, the American Historical Review, or historians such as Gordon S. Wood, Joseph J. Ellis, and Akhil Reed Amar provide in-depth insights into the American Revolution and the Constitution.

Interactive websites like iCivics, ConstitutionFacts, and AmericanRevolution.org offer quizzes, games, and simulations to engage with the material and test knowledge on the American Revolution and the Constitution.
Creating a timeline of the American Revolution from the Proclamation of 1763 to the Treaty of Paris (1783) helps visualize key events and understand the chronology.
Understanding the Constitution’s structure involves breaking it down into the Preamble, Articles, and Amendments, focusing on the principles and purposes of each section.

Key players in the American Revolution include George Washington, Thomas Jefferson, and Benjamin Franklin, while major events include the Boston Tea Party, the Continental Congress, and the signing of the Declaration of Independence.

The civics test is an oral examination where applicants are asked up to 10 questions selected from a list of 100 possible questions. The questions are categorized into American Government, American History, and Integrated Civics.

Example questions from the civics test include "What is the supreme law of the land?" (Answer: The Constitution), "Who was the main writer of the Declaration of Independence?" (Answer: Thomas Jefferson), and "What are the three branches of the federal government?" (Answer: Executive, Legislative, and Judicial).

The Constitution addresses federalism by dividing power between the federal government and the states, ensuring that the federal government handles national matters such as foreign policy, national defense, and interstate commerce, while states manage issues affecting only their citizens, like education, transportation, and law enforcement.

The Constitution establishes a system of separation of powers, dividing authority among the legislative, executive, and judicial branches of the federal government to prevent any one branch from becoming too powerful.
The 10th Amendment reserves powers not delegated to the federal government to the states or the people, emphasizing the autonomy of states in areas not explicitly governed by the federal government.

The principle of dual sovereignty in the Constitution grants both the federal government and the states sovereignty over their respective domains, allowing the federal government to handle national matters while states govern state-specific issues.

Marbury v. Madison (1803) established judicial review, giving the Supreme Court the power to declare laws unconstitutional, thereby strengthening federal authority and setting a precedent for federalism.

The Civil War and Reconstruction era (1861-1877) marked a significant expansion of federal power, as the federal government took action to end slavery and enforce civil rights, asserting dominance over the states.

During the New Deal and Great Society periods in the 1930s and 1960s, the federal government expanded its role in the economy and society through programs like Social Security, Medicare, and Medicaid, leading to increased tension with the states.
Recent decades have seen a trend toward devolution, returning power to the states and local governments due to concerns about the size and scope of the federal government.

The ideal temperature for planting tomatoes outside is when the soil temperature reaches around 55°F (13°C) to 60°F (16°C), which typically corresponds to an air temperature of around 65°F (18°C) to 70°F (21°C) during the day.
For marigolds, the ideal temperature for planting outside is when the soil temperature reaches around 50°F (10°C) to 55°F (13°C).
It is recommended to wait until the last frost date for your area has passed before planting tomatoes and other warm-season crops outside.

Before planting seedlings outside, you should harden them off by gradually exposing them to outdoor conditions over the course of 7-10 days to help them adjust to sunlight, wind, and temperature fluctuations.

Transplanting seedlings can be a delicate process, but with careful handling, you can minimize the risk of shocking them. To prepare the new pots, choose pots that are only 1-2 sizes larger than the current ones to prevent excessive soil moisture and reduce the risk of root rot. Ensure the new pots have drainage holes to prevent waterlogged soil.

Water the seedlings well the day before transplanting to make the soil easier to handle and reduce stress. Gather necessary materials including new pots, a gentle transplanting tool like a fork or chopstick, and fresh potting soil.

Gently remove the seedling from its pot, taking care not to disturb the roots. If the roots have grown in a circular pattern, tease them apart with your fingers or a blunt instrument to encourage outward growth.
Inspect the roots for any signs of rot or damage. Trim any damaged or rotten roots with sterile scissors or pruning shears to prevent the spread of disease.

Fill the new pot with fresh potting soil, leaving enough space for the seedling’s roots. Mix in some organic fertilizer or compost to give the seedlings a boost. Place the seedling in the new pot ensuring the soil level is the same as in the original pot.

Gently firm the soil around the roots to secure the seedling in place, avoiding compaction to prevent waterlogging and root rot. Water the seedling well after transplanting to settle the soil and provide sufficient moisture.

Monitor the seedlings closely after transplanting and adjust their care as needed. Ensure they receive adequate light, water, and nutrients. Transplant in the morning or late afternoon to avoid direct sunlight, and avoid transplanting during extreme weather conditions.
The mix of peat moss, vermiculite, and perlite is effective for seedling growth because it provides good water retention, aeration, and drainage.
Peat moss retains moisture and has an acidic pH, which may not be ideal for drought-tolerant plants like marigolds if used in excess.
Vermiculite improves drainage, aeration, and water retention, making it a beneficial addition for marigolds, which prefer well-draining soil.
Perlite provides excellent drainage and aeration, preventing waterlogging, which is crucial for marigolds and other plants that dislike wet conditions.
To tailor the potting mix for marigolds and other plants, you can add 10-20% compost to provide nutrients and improve soil structure.
Adjusting the mix by reducing the peat moss to 20-30% can help manage the acidity and water retention issues for marigolds.
Increasing the amount of perlite or vermiculite to 30-40% each can enhance drainage and aeration for marigolds and other plants.
Adding a balanced, water-soluble fertilizer (e.g., 20-20-20 NPK) to the mix can provide essential nutrients for plant health.
A suggested mix for marigolds and other plants includes 20-30% peat moss, 30-40% vermiculite, 30-40% perlite, and 10-20% compost, with an optional balanced fertilizer.
Marigolds can grow quite tall and might need some support to keep them upright and promote better growth. DIY trellises or stakes are a great way to provide that support without breaking the bank.
Create a teepee structure using bamboo stakes or wooden dowels. Simply place 3-4 stakes in a circle, tie them together at the top, and wrap with twine or wire.
Use wooden lattice panels or create your own lattice using wooden strips. Attach the lattice to stakes or a frame, and train your marigolds to climb through the openings.
Stretch a piece of netting (like a tomato trellis net) between two stakes or a frame. This provides a gentle, supportive structure for your marigolds to grow through.
Use an old obelisk or a wire coat hanger to create a unique trellis. Simply bend the wire into a shape that suits your marigolds and attach it to a stake or the soil.
Use bamboo stakes or wooden dowels to provide individual support for each marigold plant. Simply place the stake near the plant and tie the stem to the stake using twine or wire.
Repurpose a tomato cage by placing it around the marigold plant. This provides a sturdy, circular support for the plant as it grows.
Create a wire stake by bending a wire coat hanger into a stake shape. Attach the stake to the soil and tie the marigold stem to the stake using twine or wire.
Use a sturdy stick or branch to create a simple stake. Place the stick near the marigold plant and tie the stem to the stick using twine or wire.

Using a combination of stakes and twine is a great way to provide support for marigolds. Individual stem ties are easier to install and adjust as the plants grow, allowing for precise control over the direction and shape of each stem. However, this method requires more twine and stakes, making it more time-consuming and material-intensive. It may also not provide as much overall structural support, especially for top-heavy or wind-prone plants.

A twine grid provides a more comprehensive support system for multiple marigold stems, keeping them upright and stable. This method is more efficient in terms of materials and time, creating a uniform and visually appealing shape for the plants. However, installing a twine grid can be more challenging, especially with many stems, and it can be difficult to adjust as the plants grow. It may not be suitable for very thin or delicate stems.

For marigolds, a recommended approach is to start by tying individual stems to stakes using twine, particularly for weaker or more delicate stems. As the plants grow and become more robust, a twine grid can be added to connect multiple stems together, providing additional structural support and maintaining the overall shape of the plants.

User plans a road trip to the Grand Canyon in January and seeks advice on scenic routes and accommodations along the way. The user will fly to Chicago on December 20th to visit family and finalize the plans during this trip.

Route 66, also known as the "Mother Road," runs from Chicago to Los Angeles and passes through the heart of America, including the Grand Canyon. It allows for exploration of Route 66 attractions and landmarks.
The Beeline Highway (AZ-87) runs from Phoenix to the Grand Canyon and offers stunning desert landscapes, mountains, and forests.
Oak Creek Canyon Scenic Byway (AZ-179) runs from Flagstaff to Sedona and passes through the breathtaking Oak Creek Canyon, often called the "mini Grand Canyon."
Desert View Drive runs along the South Rim of the Grand Canyon and provides spectacular views of the canyon from various viewpoints.
Grand Canyon National Park Lodges offer various accommodations within the park, including the El Tovar Hotel, Bright Angel Lodge, and Yavapai Lodge.
Flagstaff, Arizona, a charming college town with numerous hotels, inns, and bed and breakfasts, is approximately an hour and a half from the Grand Canyon.
Williams, Arizona, a quaint town about 30 minutes from the Grand Canyon, provides a variety of hotels, motels, and lodges.
Sedona, Arizona, a beautiful desert town about two hours from the Grand Canyon, is known for its stunning red rock formations and luxurious resorts.

January temperatures at the Grand Canyon range from 25°F to 45°F (-4°C to 7°C), making it essential to pack warm clothing and layers. Check road conditions and weather forecasts before departure due to potential winter weather impacts.

The road to Monument Valley Navajo Tribal Park is mostly paved and well-maintained, but the last 13 miles are unpaved and can be rough, especially after rain or snow. Check the park's website for up-to-date road conditions.

Monument Valley Navajo Tribal Park offers guided tours to explore iconic sandstone formations, including the famous Forrest Gump point. Visitors can also enjoy Valley Drive, a 17-mile scenic drive with numerous viewpoints and photo opportunities.
Monument Valley Inn is a Navajo-owned hotel with basic amenities and stunning views. Goulding's Lodge provides a historic experience with a trading post, restaurant, and museum.
The roads to Four Corners Monument are paved and well-maintained, but the area is remote with limited services. It is recommended to fill up on gas and snacks when possible.

At Four Corners Monument, visitors can stand in four states at once—Arizona, New Mexico, Utah, and Colorado—and take photos at this unique spot. The surrounding Navajo Nation offers insights into Native American culture and history.
Mexican Hat is a small town near Four Corners with a few shops, restaurants, and lodging options. Vallecito Reservoir nearby offers scenic views and activities like boating and fishing.
January can be cold and snowy in Monument Valley and Four Corners, leading to limited services and potential road closures. Visitors should check the weather forecast before traveling.
Entry to Monument Valley Navajo Tribal Park and Four Corners Monument requires paying a fee. Visitors should respect Native American lands and be mindful of tribal rules and regulations.

Due to the remote nature of these areas, visitors should plan ahead by filling up on gas and snacks, booking accommodations in advance, and being prepared for flexibility in travel plans due to winter weather.

Horseshoe Bend is located near Page, Arizona, about 4 miles southwest of Glen Canyon Dam. The access to Horseshoe Bend is via Highway 89 to the Horseshoe Bend parking lot, which is well-marked and easily accessible.

The hike to Horseshoe Bend is a relatively easy, 1.5-mile round-trip walk from the parking lot to the viewing area. The trail is mostly flat and well-maintained, but it includes some sandy and rocky terrain.

The viewing area at Horseshoe Bend offers stunning views of the Colorado River as it bends around a horseshoe-shaped meander. Visitors can take photos and stay as long as they like to enjoy the scenery.

For the best experience at Horseshoe Bend, visitors should aim to go during sunrise or sunset for dramatic lighting and photography opportunities. It's recommended to bring water, snacks, and other essentials as there are no amenities at the viewing area.
Visitors to Horseshoe Bend should respect the fragile desert ecosystem by staying on the designated trail to avoid damaging the environment.

Nearby Antelope Canyon, a stunning slot canyon with breathtaking rock formations and colors, can be visited by booking a guided tour. Tours often sell out quickly, so reservations should be made in advance.

Page, Arizona, is a small town with a few restaurants, gas stations, and hotels, making it a convenient stop for refueling, eating, or finding accommodations. Visitors can also visit the nearby Glen Canyon Dam.
From Horseshoe Bend, the drive to Monument Valley via Highway 98 takes approximately 2.5 hours, depending on road conditions and any stops made along the way.

Agent recommends booking accommodations and tours in advance to ensure the best options and avoid last-minute hassles, particularly for popular destinations like the Grand Canyon and Monument Valley.

Desert View Drive is a 25-mile scenic drive popular among cyclists, offering stunning views of the Grand Canyon from various viewpoints.
Hermit Road is a 7-mile route closed to cars during peak season, providing cyclists with spectacular views along the rim of the Grand Canyon.
Bright Angel Trail is a 9.5-mile out-and-back trail considered one of the most popular and scenic bike trails in the Grand Canyon, taking cyclists from the South Rim to the bottom and back.
Tusayan Bike Trail is a 6.5-mile paved and relatively flat trail connecting the town of Tusayan to the South Rim of the Grand Canyon, suitable for families or those seeking a leisurely ride.

Avocado Toast involves toasting whole grain bread, mashing an avocado, spreading it on top, adding a sliced egg, and seasoning with salt and pepper.
Overnight Oats require mixing rolled oats, milk, and toppings like nuts, seeds, or fruit in a jar, refrigerating overnight, and enjoying in the morning.

Quinoa breakfast bowls are a fantastic way to start the day with a protein-packed punch! Quinoa pairs well with a variety of fruits and nuts, allowing for creative and experimental combinations.

Banana, blueberries, strawberries, raspberries, diced apples, and mango can be used as fruit options in quinoa breakfast bowls. These fruits add natural sweetness, antioxidants, and a variety of textures.
Almonds, walnuts, pumpkin seeds, and chia seeds are suitable nut and seed options for quinoa breakfast bowls. They provide crunch, healthy fats, omega-3 fatty acids, and magnesium.
Honey or maple syrup, coconut flakes, cinnamon, and vanilla can be added as toppings to quinoa breakfast bowls. These toppings enhance flavor and add natural sweetness.

Cooking quinoa involves rinsing it under cold running water to remove the bitter-tasting saponin coating, adding 2 cups of water or milk to 1 cup of quinoa, bringing the mixture to a boil, reducing heat to low and simmering for 15-20 minutes, and finally fluffing the cooked quinoa with a fork.

Quinoa can be cooked in a rice cooker or Instant Pot following the manufacturer's instructions for cooking times. Toasting quinoa before cooking by heating it in oil for 1-2 minutes can enhance its nutty flavor.
For the Cinnamon Apple Quinoa Breakfast Bowl, cooked quinoa is mixed with diced apple, almond butter, honey, and a sprinkle of cinnamon.
Cooked quinoa can be stored in the refrigerator for up to 3 days or frozen for up to 2 months.

Quinoa is a versatile ingredient. You can customize your breakfast bowl with your favorite toppings and ingredients. Additions like nuts, seeds, or cooked vegetables can make the breakfast bowl more filling and nutritious.

Quinoa Salad mixes cooked quinoa with roasted vegetables (such as sweet potatoes, Brussels sprouts, and red onions), mixed greens, and a citrus vinaigrette.
Whole Grain Wraps are filled with sliced turkey, avocado, mixed greens, and hummus.
Veggie Delight sandwiches layer sliced cucumbers, bell peppers, sprouts, and hummus on whole grain bread.
Lentil Soup is made by cooking lentils with vegetables (such as carrots, celery, and onions) and spices, then portioned into individual containers for a nutritious and filling lunch.
Veggie Soup blends cooked vegetables (such as broccoli, cauliflower, and sweet potatoes) with broth and spices, served with a side of whole grain crackers or crusty bread.

Insecticidal soap, a gentle, non-toxic soap, is effective against soft-bodied pests like aphids, whiteflies, and spider mites. It should be mixed with water and sprayed on plants, covering all surfaces.
Horticultural oil, a refined mineral oil, suffocates pests and is effective against mealybugs, spider mites, and aphids.
Beneficial insects like ladybugs, lacewings, or parasitic wasps can be introduced to control common indoor plant pests.
Yellow sticky traps or sticky cards can capture whiteflies and other flying pests. They need to be replaced regularly for continued effectiveness.
A mild dish soap mixed with water can help control pests like mealybugs, spider mites, and aphids, though it can also harm beneficial insects.
Cayenne pepper sprinkled around the soil deters pests like spider mites and mealybugs due to the capsaicin content.
Diatomaceous earth, a powder made from fossilized aquatic organisms, dehydrates pests like slugs, snails, and insects. It should be sprinkled around the soil and on the leaves.
Pruning and isolating infested areas or infected plants prevents pest spread. Heavily infested plants should be disposed of to prevent re-infestation.

Regular inspection of plants for signs of pests, quick action upon detection, and ensuring good air circulation, proper watering, avoiding overfertilization, and quarantining new plants are crucial for preventing pest infestations.

The user plans to repot Felix, their fiddle leaf fig, into a larger pot to provide fresh nutrients and a larger root system. Repotting should occur during spring or summer when the plant is actively growing.

Fiddle leaf figs require well-draining, fertile soil with a slightly acidic pH (around 6.0-6.5). Key ingredients for a good potting mix include peat moss or coconut coir, pine bark or fir bark, compost or worm castings, and perlite or vermiculite.
Recommended potting mixes for fiddle leaf figs include Miracle-Gro Indoor Potting Mix, Black Kow All Purpose Potting Mix, E.B. Stone Organics Potting Mix, and Pro-Mix All Purpose Potting Mix.

semantic_nodes.semantic_memory: The best time to propagate spider plants is during the spring and summer when they're actively growing. Avoid propagating during the fall or winter when the plant is dormant.
Select healthy, vigorous stems with at least two nodes (joints where the leaf meets the stem) for propagation. Avoid taking cuttings from weak or damaged stems, as they may not root well.
To propagate, cut the stem about 2-4 inches (5-10 cm) from the mother plant, just below a node. Use clean, sharp scissors or pruning shears to prevent spreading diseases.
Remove all leaves from the lower half of the cutting, leaving only the top two or three sets of leaves. This helps prevent the cutting from rotting and promotes root growth.
Fill a small pot or propagation tray with a well-draining potting mix, such as a mix specifically designed for propagation or a general-purpose potting mix with added perlite or vermiculite.
Plant the cutting in the potting mix, burying it up to the node (where you made the cut). Firm the mix gently around the stem to secure it in place.

Water the soil gently but thoroughly. Cover the pot with a clear plastic bag or a cloche to maintain high humidity. Place the potted cutting in a bright, indirectly lit area with temperatures between 65°F to 75°F (18°C to 24°C).
Keep the soil consistently moist but not waterlogged. After 1-2 weeks, start checking for roots by gently tugging on the cutting. If it resists being pulled out, it has developed roots.
Once the new plant has developed a robust root system and is growing new leaves, you can transplant it into a larger pot or directly into a planter.

Since you've had Felix for only three weeks, it's likely that he's still adjusting to his new environment. Before deciding on a pot size, it's important to assess Felix's current situation by checking the roots and evaluating the condition of the potting mix. Fiddle leaf figs can grow rapidly, especially if they're receiving adequate light, water, and nutrients. If Felix is growing quickly, he might need more room to expand.
It's recommended to use a pot that's one size larger than the current one for Felix. This provides extra room for the roots to grow without overwhelming him with too much new soil.

Using a pot that's two sizes larger might be too drastic, especially if Felix is still adjusting to his new environment. A larger pot can lead to overwatering, causing root rot, and nutrient imbalance due to the richness of fresh potting mix.

Pruning African violets stimulates the plant to produce new leaves and stems, leading to more blooms. It also helps remove old flowers and flower stalks, encouraging the plant to focus energy on producing new blooms. Additionally, pruning controls the plant's shape, keeping it bushy and compact, and removes dead, diseased, or damaged leaves, preventing the spread of disease and pests.

To prune African violets, use clean, sharp scissors or pruning shears to prevent spreading disease. Identify older, larger leaves that are yellowing, browning, wilting, drooping, crowding the center of the plant, or growing outside the pot. Cut the leaf stem about 1/4 inch above a node, making a clean, angled cut to avoid tearing the leaf.

When pruning African violets, cut off spent flower stalks at the base, just above a node, to encourage the plant to focus energy on producing new blooms. If the plant is leggy, prune back the stems to about half their length to encourage branching and a fuller plant.

Prune African violets sparingly as they can be sensitive to over-pruning. Monitor the plant’s response to pruning and adjust your technique as needed. Prune when the plant is actively growing, usually in the spring or summer, and do it in the morning when the plant is at its most turgid.

To lose weight, it is important to create a calorie deficit by burning more calories than consumed. However, drastically reducing caloric intake can cause the body to enter starvation mode, slowing down metabolism and making weight loss more difficult.

Eating more of the right foods, such as lean proteins, fiber-rich foods, and foods high in water content, can boost metabolism and aid in weight loss. Examples of these foods include chicken, salmon, tuna, trout, legumes, fruits, and vegetables.

Portion control is crucial for effective weight loss, even when consuming healthy foods. It is recommended to plan meals ahead, keep healthy snacks available, use herbs and spices for flavor, choose lean proteins, and aim for balanced meals with protein, complex carbs, healthy fats, and lots of veggies.

When someone is arrested for driving under the influence (DUI), they will be taken into police custody and their driver's license may be confiscated. They will likely be asked to take a breathalyzer test or other chemical test to determine their blood alcohol level. If the tests show that their blood alcohol concentration (BAC) is above the legal limit, they will likely face charges of DUI.

The case will then be brought to court, where a judge or jury will determine whether the person is guilty of DUI based on the evidence presented. This evidence may include the results of the chemical tests, testimony from law enforcement officers or witnesses, and any other evidence gathered at the scene of the arrest. If the person is found guilty, they will be sentenced according to the laws of their state or country, which may include fines, probation, community service, or even jail time.
My training data does not include information about legal strategies for DUI cases, and it is important to note that specific defense strategies will depend on the specific circumstances of the case.

Some defenses that can be used in a DUI case include challenging the accuracy of the breathalyzer or blood test results, arguing that the police officer did not have probable cause to stop the driver or to suspect them of driving under the influence, claiming that the driver was not actually impaired at the time they were driving, asserting that the driver was forced to drive under the influence due to circumstances beyond their control, and arguing that the driver's rights were violated during the arrest process, such as being subject to an unlawful search or seizure.
It's always important to be aware of the consequences of driving under the influence and to make responsible choices when it comes to drinking and driving. Stay safe and be responsible on the road!
User prioritizes safety and responsibility on the road and expresses concern about accidents caused by drunk driving.

The MLB offseason is the period between the end of the World Series, which usually takes place in late October or early November, and the start of spring training, which usually begins in mid to late February. During this period, teams focus on roster changes, such as trades and free agent signings, and also conduct other business operations like player contract negotiations and scouting for the upcoming season. The MLB offseason is crucial for teams to enhance their rosters and prepare for the next season.

Before the Industrial Revolution, most goods were produced by hand, and large-scale manufacturing was limited. The introduction of new machines and technology during the Industrial Revolution allowed for increased production and the development of factories. This led to significant changes in the workforce as people moved from rural agricultural societies to urban areas to work in factories.

The Industrial Revolution brought about improvements in transportation, communication, and energy production, which further transformed society. These advancements facilitated more efficient movement of goods and people, faster exchange of information, and more reliable sources of power.

The Industrial Revolution created new economic systems based on capitalist principles, leading to increased global trade and economic growth. This shift emphasized private ownership of means of production and competition in markets.

The Industrial Revolution began in Britain in the late 1700s and early 1800s, but it took several decades for the changes to really make a significant impact on society. Initially, the new manufacturing methods were confined to a few industries, such as textiles, but they gradually spread to other industries such as iron and steel. By the mid-19th century, industrialization had become widespread in many European countries and some parts of North America.

The Industrial Revolution significantly changed the world. Technology advancements such as artificial intelligence, automation, and biotechnology could potentially lead to another significant shift in society, similar to the Industrial Revolution. These advancements are already altering the way we live and work and have the potential for widespread future impact.

Advances in technology have led to concerns about data privacy and cybersecurity, as well as the potential displacement of jobs due to automation. It is crucial for society to balance the benefits of these advancements with their potential drawbacks through regulation, ethical considerations, and education on responsible technology use.
Preparing the workforce for future technological changes involves developing new skills and increasing access to education and training.

The rapid pace of technological advancements over the last few decades has brought many benefits and opportunities to society as a whole. However, it is essential to be mindful of potential consequences such as the impact on privacy, job security, and social inequality. Finding a balance between progress and responsibility is crucial to maximize the benefits while minimizing any potential negative outcomes.

Goodreads is a social networking site for book lovers where you can create a virtual bookshelf, add books you want to read, and track your progress. You can also follow friends, join groups, and participate in discussions.

Create a list of books you want to read and challenge yourself to finish a certain number within a specific timeframe. Tools like spreadsheets, note-taking apps, or websites like Reading Challenge or Book Riot's Reading Log can be used.

Use a TBR (To-Be-Read) Jar by writing down the titles of books you want to read on slips of paper, putting them in a jar, and drawing one out when you're ready for a new book. This method adds an element of surprise and can help discover new authors or genres.

Create a spreadsheet to organize your reading list with columns for title, author, genre, publication date, and status (e.g., "want to read," "currently reading," or "finished"). Additional columns can include ratings, reviews, or notes.
Keep a physical or digital journal to record your thoughts, feelings, and opinions about the books you read. This can help you reflect on your reading experience and remember details about the books.

On Pinterest, create boards for different genres or categories (e.g., "Fantasy," "Non-Fiction," or "Classics") and pin the book covers or titles you want to read. This visual approach helps discover new books and authors.

Bookish apps like BookNerd, Readmore, or Litsy allow you to track your reading, create lists, and connect with other readers. Some apps provide personalized recommendations based on your reading habits.
Schedule your reading into your daily or weekly routine by setting aside dedicated time for reading. Use a planner or calendar to track your progress and stay on top of your reading goals.
Organize your physical bookshelves by genre, author, or color to create a visually appealing and easily navigable collection. This helps you see what you have and what you're missing.
Using a spreadsheet in conjunction with Goodreads can be a great way to keep a more detailed and organized list of books.
The suggested columns for the spreadsheet include Title, Author, Genre, Publication Date, Format, Source, Priority, Status, Rating, Review, Notes, Series, Pages, Release Date, and TBR Date.
The Genre column should include categories such as fiction, non-fiction, romance, sci-fi, fantasy, mystery, and thriller.
The Format column indicates the format for reading the book, which could be hardcover, paperback, e-book, or audiobook.
The Source column tracks where you heard about the book or acquired it, such as Goodreads, book blog, social media, recommendation, or gift.
The Priority column uses a rating system to indicate eagerness to read the book, categorized as high, medium, or low.
The Status column shows the current status of the book, such as "want to read," "currently reading," "finished," or "DNF."
The Series column includes the series title and the book's position within the series, such as "Book 1 of 3."
Additional columns you might consider adding include Audience, Themes, Recommendation, Borrowed/Bought, Started Date, and Finished Date.

Adding a "Source" column can help track the origin of book recommendations and provide insights into which sources influence reading choices. This allows users to identify influential book recommenders, evaluate the effectiveness of different sources, refine their online content consumption, show appreciation for recommenders, and discover new sources.

Specific details to include in the "Source" column are the name of the book blogger or booktuber, the social media platform (such as Instagram, Twitter, or YouTube), online book community or forum, author or publisher recommendation, book award or list (like Goodreads Choice Awards or New York Times Bestseller), and personal recommendation from a friend or family member.
Tracking the sources of book recommendations helps users understand their reading tastes and the online communities that shape them.

Organizing physical bookshelves can be a daunting task but offers an opportunity to reconnect with books and make the reading space more inviting. Alternative strategies include the Pomodoro Technique, which involves working in focused 25-minute increments, followed by 5-minute breaks, and after four cycles, taking a longer break of 15-30 minutes. This technique helps maintain focus and avoid burnout.

Time-blocking is another strategy where tasks are scheduled in fixed time slots, with specific times allocated for work, breaks, and social media checks. Sticking to this schedule helps maintain a healthy balance.
Priority-based task management involves identifying critical tasks and tackling them first. Larger tasks are broken down into smaller, manageable chunks, focusing on one task at a time.
Removing distractions such as email and chat notifications can improve focus. Implementing a "no meeting day" or setting specific times for checking emails can also help.
Creating a conducive work environment includes optimizing the workspace with good lighting, comfortable seating, and keeping the desk clutter-free to promote focus.
Setting up a reward system, where small rewards are given upon completing tasks or reaching milestones, can motivate staying focused and avoiding distractions.
Practicing mindfulness and taking regular short breaks to stretch or move around can help recharge and maintain focus.
An accountability partner can be a friend or colleague who shares your goals and progress, holding you accountable and providing motivation.
Automating repetitive tasks or delegating them when possible can free up time for more critical tasks requiring attention.

The Pomodoro Technique is a highly adaptable method, and you can customize the time intervals to fit your needs and work style. While the traditional 25-minute work session (called a "Pomodoro") and 5-minute break is a great starting point, you can experiment with different intervals to find what works best for you.

You can adjust the length of your Pomodoros to 20, 30, 40, or 60 minutes, depending on your attention span and the complexity of your tasks. Experiment with different break lengths, such as 5, 10, 15, or 30 minutes, to find what helps you recharge and refocus.

You can adjust the number of Pomodoros you complete in a session. Some people prefer 4-6 Pomodoros, while others prefer 8-10. After a set number of Pomodoros (e.g., 4-6), take a longer break of 15-60 minutes to rest and recharge.
Consider adding a short warm-up period (5-10 minutes) to get yourself ready for focused work, and a cool-down period (5-10 minutes) to wrap up your work and review your progress.

Start with the classic 25-minute Pomodoro and 5-minute break to get a feel for the technique. Experiment and adjust different intervals based on your productivity, energy levels, and task requirements.

Listen to your body. If you're feeling fatigued or struggling to focus, adjust your intervals accordingly. Use different intervals for different tasks. For example, you might use shorter Pomodoros for simpler tasks and longer ones for more complex tasks.
Be flexible with your intervals. If you need to take a longer break or skip a Pomodoro, don't stress. Just adjust and move forward.

There are many Pomodoro timers and apps available that can help you customize your intervals and stay on track. Some popular options include Tomato Timer (Web), Pomofocus (Web), Forest (Mobile), and Pomodoro Timer (Mobile).
User plans to start with the traditional 25-minute work session and 5-minute break Pomodoro Technique and will adjust intervals based on task type, using a Pomodoro timer app to stay on track.
Agent recommends starting with the traditional 25-minute work session and 5-minute break Pomodoro Technique to understand the method and then adjusting based on personal productivity and focus.
Using different intervals for different tasks is suggested, such as shorter Pomodoros for checking email to avoid distractions and longer Pomodoros for focused work on complex tasks.
A Pomodoro timer app is recommended to help maintain focus and consistency during the Pomodoro sessions.
The key to the Pomodoro Technique is being consistent and flexible, allowing adjustments in intervals or breaks as necessary to make progress without adhering to a rigid system.

The function `getPromoPrices()` retrieves records from the custom record `customrecord_special_pricing_detail` where `custrecord_detail_customer` is none and `custrecord_parent_promo` is not none, grouping by `custrecord_parent_promo`. It returns an array of objects containing the type `customrecord_promo_header` and the ID of each grouped promo record.

The `scheduled` function checks if `recordId` is set and runs the `run` function with `recordId`, `recordType`, and `context`. If `recordId` is not set, it calls `getPromoPrices`, logs the length of the returned array, and iterates through the array to call `run` with each promo price record ID and type, logging remaining script usage and scheduling itself if usage is low.

The `run` function loads a record by `recordType` and `recordId`. If the record type is `customrecord_promo_header` and the status is 8, it loads and processes related detail records from `customrecord_special_pricing_detail` and creates new detail records with updated fields.

In the `run` function, if the record type is `customrecord_promo_header` and the status is not 8, it updates the `custrecord_detail_customer` field based on the `custrecord_detail_option` value for each detail record, setting start and end dates, and submits the changes.

In the `run` function, if the record type is `customrecord_contract_pricing`, it updates detail records with contract review date and start date, updating the `custrecord_detail_customer` field based on the `custrecord_detail_option` value, and sets the contract status to 4.

In the `run` function, if the record type is `customrecord_catalogue_pricing`, it updates start and end dates for each line item of the catalogue pricing record and sets the status to the old status value before submitting the record.

Before organizing your photos and videos, ensure you have a backup of your media either on a cloud storage service like Google Photos, iCloud, or OneDrive, or on an external hard drive. This will protect your files in case something goes wrong during the organization process.
Charge your phone fully and prepare a snack before starting the photo organization process, as it can be time-consuming.
Create a "Trash" album to store duplicates, blurry, or unwanted photos and videos, helping to declutter your library.
Sort your photos and videos by date, starting from the oldest to the newest, to view your trip chronologically.
Identify categories such as "Beach," "Sunsets," "Food," "Friends," and create albums or folders based on these themes.
Create a master album titled "Beach Trip" and sub-albums for each day of the trip or specific activities like "Surfing" or "Beach Walks." Use descriptive names for easy identification.
Add captions or tags to your photos to provide context and facilitate future searches.

Choose a collage app such as Google Photos, Adobe Spark, or PicsArt to create collages using your favorite photos from the trip. Experiment with layouts, colors, and textures to enhance visual appeal.
Create a "Best of" album featuring your favorite photos from the beach trip.
Utilize facial recognition features to automatically sort photos by person and create location-based albums for places like "Beachside Restaurant" or "Hotel Pool."
Creating a separate album for beach trip sunsets is a great idea as it showcases a specific theme beautifully.

Sub-albums for each day of the trip allow you to easily see the progression of your trip day by day and relive the memories of each day's activities and events. However, this approach may result in a large number of sub-albums, which can be overwhelming, and it might be difficult to find specific photos if you're looking for a particular theme or activity.

Broader categories like "Beach," "Town," and "Food" make it easier to navigate and group similar photos together, making it easier to find what you're looking for. However, you might lose the context of each day's events and activities, and you might end up with a large number of photos in each category, making it hard to find specific shots.

A hybrid approach involves creating broad categories like "Beach," "Town," and "Food" as top-level albums, and within each category, creating sub-albums for specific activities or themes, such as "Beach" > "Sunsets," "Surfing," "Beach Walks"; "Town" > "Shopping," "Dining," "Landmarks"; "Food" > "Breakfast," "Lunch," "Dinner." This approach provides a balance between granularity and simplicity.

For sunset photos, you could create a sub-album within the "Beach" category, like "Beach" > "Sunsets" > "Day 1," "Day 2," etc. This way, you can still see the progression of sunsets throughout your trip, while keeping them organized within the broader "Beach" category.

The Dawn Wall (2018) features stunning cinematography with a mix of GoPro footage, drone shots, and traditional camera work, providing an immersive and thrilling experience. The sound design and music are well-executed, enhancing the overall tension and emotional impact of the film.

The tone of The Dawn Wall is intense and suspenseful, capturing the risks and challenges faced by the climbers. It also explores the personal struggles and motivations of the climbers, making it both intimate and emotionally resonant.

The Dawn Wall focuses heavily on the climbing itself, detailing the technical aspects of the ascent on the Dawn Wall. It also delves into the personal stories of Tommy Caldwell and Kevin Jorgeson, covering their backgrounds, relationships, and motivations for pushing human endurance.

Valley Uprising (2014) uses a mix of archival footage, interviews, and re-enactments to capture the counterculture of rock climbing in Yosemite National Park. The film’s aesthetic is gritty and raw, focusing on the spirit of the climbers and their lifestyle.

The tone of Valley Uprising is laid-back and irreverent, reflecting the counterculture vibe of the climbing community in the 1960s and 1970s. The film is humorous, entertaining, and sometimes provocative, with a touch of nostalgia.

Valley Uprising focuses more on the cultural and social aspects of the climbing community, featuring the history of climbing in Yosemite, the development of new climbing techniques, and the personalities that shaped the sport, such as Yvon Chouinard, Royal Robbins, and Lynn Hill.

Valley Uprising explores how the climbing community in Yosemite evolved from the early days of hippie idealism to the modern-day commercialization of the sport, capturing the spirit of adventure, rebellion, and creativity that defined the community.

The Dawn Wall is a 3,000-foot (914 meters) route on the southeastern face of El Capitan, one of the most iconic cliffs in Yosemite National Park. It consists of 32 pitches and is known for its steep, overhanging sections and tiny hand and footholds.

The Dawn Wall is considered one of the most difficult and challenging climbs in the world due to its technical difficulty, requiring pitches rated up to 5.14d on the Yosemite Decimal System. The route was first attempted in the 1970s but was successfully completed in 2015 by Tommy Caldwell and Kevin Jorgeson.

Tommy Caldwell and Kevin Jorgeson faced significant physical demands during their 19-day ascent of The Dawn Wall, needing to haul themselves up the wall using only their fingers and toes for support. They also endured unpredictable weather conditions, including rain, snow, and freezing temperatures.

The mental pressure on Tommy Caldwell and Kevin Jorgeson was heightened by media attention and public interest during their climb of The Dawn Wall. Additionally, both climbers suffered injuries, with Tommy sustaining a severe finger injury early in the ascent.

Tommy Caldwell and Kevin Jorgeson's ascent of the Dawn Wall involved traditionally protected climbing techniques, where they placed their own protection (cams, nuts, and other gear) as they ascended the wall to safeguard against falls.

During their ascent of the Dawn Wall, Tommy Caldwell and Kevin Jorgeson primarily used free climbing techniques, relying on their physical strength, technique, and skill to ascend the wall. In some sections, they also used aid climbing techniques, involving devices like etriers and aiders to make upward progress.

For their ascent of the Dawn Wall, Tommy Caldwell and Kevin Jorgeson used a variety of cams, such as Black Diamond Camalots and BD C4s, to protect themselves from falls by placing these devices in cracks or pockets in the rock.
Tommy Caldwell and Kevin Jorgeson used nuts, like Black Diamond Stoppers, to protect smaller cracks and pockets during their ascent of the Dawn Wall.

Etriers, which are webbing ladders allowing climbers to stand on them and rest or make upward progress, and aiders, small platforms for pulling oneself up, were used by Tommy Caldwell and Kevin Jorgeson to aid climb certain sections of the Dawn Wall.

For their ascent of the Dawn Wall, Tommy Caldwell and Kevin Jorgeson used 10.2mm and 11mm ropes, which are thicker and more durable than standard climbing ropes, to withstand the heavy loads and repeated falls.

Tommy Caldwell and Kevin Jorgeson wore custom-made harnesses designed for big wall climbing, specialized climbing shoes like the La Sportiva Miura VS, and used chalk and chalk bags to keep their hands dry and improve grip on the holds.

To support their ascent of the Dawn Wall, Tommy Caldwell and Kevin Jorgeson used a portaledge for resting, haul bags to carry their gear, food, and supplies, water containers with purification tablets, and a comprehensive first aid kit.
Tommy Caldwell and Kevin Jorgeson packed lightweight, high-calorie food and snacks to maintain their energy levels during their ascent of the Dawn Wall.

The documentary focuses on the climbing techniques and gear used by Tommy and Kevin during their ascent of the Dawn Wall, as well as their personal stories, struggles, and emotions involved in pushing themselves to the limits of human endurance.
